papers.title: Integrative bioinformatics and experimental analysis identify S100A10 as a diagnostic and prognostic biomarker in pancreatic adenocarcinoma.
papers.abstract: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma (PAAD) is an extremely invasive malignancy with limited effective treatment options. S100 calcium-binding protein A10 (S100A10), a protein-coding gene, plays a critical role in tumor progression. However, its correlation with immune cell infiltration and PAAD prognosis has not been previously reported. This study aimed to systematically explore the expression pattern, biological function and clinical value of S100A10 in PAAD.
papers.summary: This study identifies S100A10 as a potential diagnostic and prognostic biomarker for pancreatic adenocarcinoma through integrative bioinformatics and experimental analysis. The findings suggest S100A10 is associated with immune cell infiltration and tumor progression, offering a new target for diagnosis and treatment.

papers.title: Crystalline silica particles exposure modulated miRNA expression profiles in human macrophage-like THP-1 cells.
papers.abstract: Occupational and environmental exposure to crystalline silica particles is a major global health concern linked to silicosis, pulmonary fibrosis, autoimmune disease, and lung cancer. MicroRNAs (miRNAs) regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally and are emerging as potential effect biomarkers of exposure in human biomonitoring studies. To identify intracellular and extracellular miRNAs responses to silica-particle exposure in human THP-1 derived macrophages, differentiated THP-1 derived macrophages were exposed to 0-300 µg/mL crystalline silica particles for 24 h. Cytotoxicity was assessed
papers.summary: This study investigates how exposure to crystalline silica particles alters microRNA (miRNA) expression in human macrophage-like cells, identifying potential biomarkers for silica-related diseases. The researchers exposed THP-1 derived macrophages to varying concentrations of silica and found changes in both intracellular and extracellular miRNA profiles, which may help understand the molecular mechanisms of silicosis and other lung diseases.

papers.title: Molecular Safeguards of Survival: De novo transcriptome assembly and tissue-specific transcriptomic profiling of the yellow-foot clam Paphia malabarica.
papers.abstract: The yellow-foot clam, Paphia malabarica Chemnitz, 1782 is a regionally important fishery and aquaculture resource along the southwest coast of India. The fishery of this species from the Ashtamudi estuary has been certified as sustainable by the Marine Stewardship Council. We generated the first high-quality, comprehensive de novo transcriptome assembly of Paphia malabarica using Illumina paired-end sequencing. A total of 729.2 million raw reads were generated and further processed and assembled using the Trinity assembler. The final assembly yielded 55,980 predicted coding sequences (CDS) wit
papers.summary: This study presents the first high-quality de novo transcriptome assembly for the yellow-foot clam, a sustainably fished species in India. By sequencing and assembling over 729 million reads, the authors identified nearly 56,000 coding sequences, providing a valuable genomic resource for understanding the biology and conservation of this economically important bivalve.

papers.title: A single-cell transcriptomic atlas of the pigtail macaque placenta in late gestation.
papers.abstract: The placenta is a complex organ with multiple immune and non-immune cell types that promote fetal tolerance and facilitate the transfer of nutrients and oxygen. The nonhuman primate (NHP) is a key experimental model for studying human pregnancy complications, in part due to similarities in placental structure, which makes it essential to understand how single-cell populations compare across the human and NHP maternal-fetal interface. We constructed a single-cell RNA-Seq (scRNA-Seq) atlas of the placenta from the pigtail macaque (Macaca nemestrina) in the third trimester, comprising three diffe
papers.summary: This study presents a single-cell RNA sequencing atlas of the pigtail macaque placenta in late gestation, identifying distinct immune and non-immune cell populations. The resource enables comparative analysis with the human maternal-fetal interface, highlighting similarities and differences that are important for understanding pregnancy complications in a relevant nonhuman primate model.

papers.title: Multi-omics analysis of extracellular vesicle cargo in cancer.
papers.abstract: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are increasingly recognized as minimally invasive sources of molecular information in cancer, carrying nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, and metabolites that reflect tumor biology. Recent advances in high-throughput sequencing and mass spectrometry have not only enabled multi-omics profiling of EV cargo but also revealed that different molecular layers capture distinct yet complementary aspects of cancer processes. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the rapidly expanding landscape of EV multi-omics, highlighting methodological constraints, the predomin
papers.summary: This review discusses how extracellular vesicles (EVs) from tumors carry diverse molecular cargo (nucleic acids, proteins, lipids, metabolites) that can be profiled using multi-omics technologies to gain complementary insights into cancer biology. It highlights the potential of EV-based liquid biopsies as minimally invasive diagnostic tools, while also addressing current methodological limitations.

papers.title: MIND: multimodal integration with neighbourhood-aware distributions.
papers.abstract: Multimodal data integration combines different data modalities to improve predictive and classification performance. In biology, multi-omics profiling has become a powerful tool for applications such as cancer patient stratification. However, integration of multi-omics data remains challenging because of missingness and inherent heterogeneity. Methods such as imputation and sample exclusion often rely on strong assumptions that could lead to information loss or distortion. To address these limitations, we propose MIND (Multimodal Integration with Neighbourhood-aware Distributions), which learn
papers.summary: MIND is a new method for integrating different types of biological data (multi-omics) that handles missing data and heterogeneity better than existing approaches. It learns neighborhood-aware distributions to combine modalities without relying on strong imputation assumptions, improving cancer patient stratification.

papers.title: A pragmatist approach to bridging tables and ontologies through LinkML and punning.
papers.abstract: Ontologies support data integration and knowledge discovery across multiple life science disciplines through the application of formal semantics that define hierarchical and cross-hierarchical relationships which enable automated reasoning capabilities. At the same time, tabular data is by far the most commonly produced format of data in most disciplines. This is especially true in biodiversity dataset production. Creating rich semantic data from tables can be complex because of a mismatch between flat tabular data and hierarchical ontology models. Here, we present a practical approach for clo
papers.summary: This paper presents a practical method using LinkML and punning to bridge the gap between flat tabular data and hierarchical ontology models, making it easier to create rich semantic data from tables. The approach is particularly relevant for biodiversity datasets, where tabular formats are common but semantic integration is challenging.

papers.title: Vitiligo Information Resource database v3.
papers.abstract: Vitiligo is a multifaceted autoimmune skin disease characterized by the loss of epidermal melanocytes, yet its molecular basis remains poorly understood. To address the need for a specialized systems biology interface, we present VIRdb v3.0, an integrative database combining curated transcriptomics-based datasets with differentially expressed genes, a protein-protein interaction network highlighting regulatory hubs, and an enriched library of natural compounds and FDA-approved drugs targeting vitiligo-related proteins. The platform supports pathway enrichment, molecular docking, and cross-dise
papers.summary: The Vitiligo Information Resource database v3.0 integrates curated transcriptomics data, protein-protein interaction networks, and drug libraries to provide a systems biology platform for studying vitiligo. It enables pathway enrichment and molecular docking analyses, facilitating the identification of regulatory hubs and potential therapeutic compounds.

papers.title: Investigation of potential targets and mechanisms of naringenin in the treatment of spinal cord injury: A network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulation study.
papers.abstract: Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a disease that causes significant functional impairment and high mortality, imposing a heavy economic burden on patients and society. In this in-silico study, we investigated the potential therapeutic targets and underlying mechanisms of naringenin (NAR) in SCI by integrating network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation. The intersection of NAR and SCI targets was used to construct a protein-protein interaction network using the STRING database. Gene Ontology, Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes, and Wiki Pathway enrichment ana
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods (network pharmacology, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations) to identify potential therapeutic targets and mechanisms of naringenin for treating spinal cord injury. The findings suggest naringenin may act through multiple proteins and pathways, but the work is entirely in silico with no experimental validation.

papers.title: Spatial omics illuminates tumor heterogeneity.
papers.abstract: Intratumoral heterogeneity (ITH) is the coexistence of diverse cancer cell states, genotypes, and microenvironmental niches within a single tumor and is a major driver of therapeutic resistance and disease progression. While the clinical implications of ITH are well appreciated, conventional methods and models have been limited in resolving the spatial and functional complexity underlying ITH. Recent advances in single-cell and spatial omics, high-plex imaging, and in vivo CRISPR perturbation and lineage tracing now enable unprecedented, mechanistic dissection of tumor ecosystems. These techno
papers.summary: This review discusses how intratumoral heterogeneity (ITH)—the diversity of cancer cells and their microenvironment within a single tumor—drives therapy resistance and disease progression. It highlights how recent advances in single-cell and spatial omics, high-plex imaging, and in vivo CRISPR tools now allow researchers to dissect the spatial and functional complexity of tumor ecosystems at unprecedented resolution.

papers.title: Protocol for integrating and interpreting multi-omics data combining unsupervised and supervised data integrating approaches.
papers.abstract: Multi-omics integration combines data from transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics to provide insights into biological systems. Here, we present a protocol for integrating and interpreting multi-omics data using unsupervised multi-omics factor analysis (MOFA), supervised projection-based integration (data integration analysis for biomarker discovery using latent components, DIABLO), along with general single-omics analysis techniques and visualizations. We describe steps for data preparation, model construction, and biological interpretation of multi-omics datasets. These approaches iden
papers.summary: This protocol describes a step-by-step approach for integrating transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics data using both unsupervised (MOFA) and supervised (DIABLO) methods. It covers data preparation, model building, and biological interpretation, enabling researchers to extract meaningful insights from multi-omics datasets.

papers.title: MetaHarmonizer: robust biomedical metadata harmonization and a contamination control for inflated LLM performance on public benchmarks.
papers.abstract: Public biomedical repositories hold substantial reuse potential, but inconsistent metadata routinely blocks integration across studies. Recent LLM-based harmonization approaches address scale but suffer from non-determinism, hallucinated ontology terms, and, in their highest-accuracy configurations, dependence on proprietary APIs or labeled fine-tuning data. A more fundamental concern is that LLM accuracies on widely-used public benchmarks may substantially inflate transferable capability: under a contamination-controlled evaluation protocol we developed, the apparent LLM-only advantage on the
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper introduces MetaHarmonizer, a tool for robust biomedical metadata harmonization that addresses key limitations of LLM-based approaches, including non-determinism, hallucinated ontology terms, and dependence on proprietary APIs. The authors also develop a contamination-controlled evaluation protocol revealing that LLM performance on public benchmarks may be substantially inflated, questioning the true transferable capability of these models.

papers.title: Cutibacterium acnes induce T cell extracellular traps with distinct proteome programs.
papers.abstract: Cutibacterium acnes (C. acnes) is a common skin commensal associated in acne pathogenesis. Here, we investigated whether acne-associated (HL043PA1, CA) and healthy skin-associated (HL110PA3, CH) C. acnes strains differentially induce extracellular traps (ET) in human neutrophils (NETs) and Th17 cells (TETs) and defined their proteomic and functional properties. Both strains triggered rapid ET release from neutrophils and Th17 cells. However, whereas NETs displayed a conserved proteomic profile regardless of strain, TETs exhibited strain-specific specialization. CA-induced TETs were enriched in
papers.summary: This study shows that different strains of the skin bacterium Cutibacterium acnes trigger the formation of extracellular traps from immune cells, but with distinct proteomic profiles: while neutrophil traps are similar regardless of strain, Th17 cell traps are specialized depending on the bacterial strain, revealing a new layer of host-microbe interaction in acne.

papers.title: Next-Generation Bionic Sensors for Small Molecule Detection: Integrating Synthetic Biology, Nanomaterials, and Artificial Intelligence.
papers.abstract: Bionic sensors are emerging as powerful analytical platforms driving the development of next-generation detection technologies, particularly for small molecule sensing in complex environmental and biological systems. However, accurate and selective detection of small molecules remains fundamentally challenging due to their low molecular weight, limited structural specificity, and strong interference from complex matrices. This review provides a comprehensive overview of recent advances in bionic sensor technologies, focusing on how the integration of synthetic biology, nanomaterials, and artif
papers.summary: This review discusses how combining synthetic biology, nanomaterials, and artificial intelligence can improve bionic sensors for detecting small molecules, which are typically difficult to measure accurately due to their size and interference from complex samples. It covers recent advances in these integrated sensor technologies for applications in environmental and biological systems.

papers.title: Bioinformatics analysis identifies a novel 4-gene signature associated with immune cell infiltration in osteoarthritis: a combined bioinformatics and experimental validation study.
papers.abstract: Osteoarthritis is the most common degenerative joint condition, yet its pathogenesis remains incompletely understood. This study aims to identify key differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in osteoarthritis-affected synovial tissues and investigate potential underlying immune mechanisms using bioinformatics approaches. Gene expression datasets GSE12021, GSE55235, and GSE55457 from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) were analyzed, comprising 30 osteoarthritis and 28 normal synovial tissue samples. Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified using the limma R package. Functional enrichme
papers.summary: This study analyzed gene expression data from osteoarthritis patients and healthy controls to identify a 4-gene signature linked to immune cell infiltration in the joint. The findings suggest that these genes may play a role in the immune-related aspects of osteoarthritis, potentially offering new targets for diagnosis or therapy.

papers.title: VITAGRAPH: building a knowledge graph for biologically relevant learning tasks.
papers.abstract: The complexity of human biology poses ongoing challenges, driving global interdisciplinary research. Artificial intelligence has become a powerful tool in computational biology, where graph data structures model entities like protein-protein interaction (PPI) networks and gene functional networks. These networks support crucial tasks in network medicine, including gene-disease association prediction, drug repurposing, and polypharmacy side-effect analysis. Reliable machine learning predictions require high-quality data. We present VITAGRAPH, a comprehensive multi-purpose biological knowledge g
papers.summary: VITAGRAPH is a new biological knowledge graph that integrates diverse data types (e.g., protein-protein interactions, gene functions) to support machine learning tasks like gene-disease prediction and drug repurposing. It provides high-quality, structured data for training reliable AI models in network medicine.

papers.title: KG-Microbe - Building Modular and Scalable Knowledge Graphs for Microbiome and Microbial Sciences.
papers.abstract: The integration of many disparate forms of data is essential for understanding the microbial world and its interaction with the environment and human health. Doing so is particularly challenging in the context of microbe-host and microbe-microbe interactions that contribute to health or environmental outcomes. There are thousands of relevant microbial species, and millions of interactions among those microbes and with their environment or host. Integrated information (e.g., about host and microbial physiology, genetics, and metabolism) facilitates deeper understanding of complex mechanisms and
papers.summary: KG-Microbe is a new framework for building modular, scalable knowledge graphs that integrate diverse microbial data (e.g., genetics, metabolism, host interactions) to better understand complex microbe-host and microbe-environment relationships. It addresses the challenge of handling millions of interactions across thousands of microbial species by providing a structured, reusable approach to data integration. This resource aims to accelerate research in microbiome science by enabling more comprehensive and interconnected analyses.

papers.title: Multimodal temporal feature fusion for teacher competency assessment and precision training resource recommendation.
papers.abstract: Assessing teacher competency in a reliable and multidimensional manner remains an open problem, largely because conventional evaluation instruments capture only a fraction of the behavioral repertoire that defines effective instruction. We tackle this challenge by developing an integrated framework that fuses heterogeneous classroom signals-video, audio, transcribed text, and physiological recordings-through modality-specific encoders coupled with a cross-modal attention mechanism. The attention module adaptively re-weights each data stream according to its diagnostic relevance for a given com
papers.summary: This paper presents a multimodal AI framework that integrates video, audio, text, and physiological data from classrooms to assess teacher competency more comprehensively than traditional methods. The system uses cross-modal attention to dynamically weigh each data stream's relevance, enabling both reliable assessment and personalized training recommendations.

papers.title: Multimodal genomic prediction is not a buzzword: why modern plant breeding must integrate genomics, enviromics, and phenomics.
papers.abstract: Plant breeding has entered a new era, transitioning from a predominantly unimodal, genomics-focused paradigm to one defined by multimodal data integration. Dense genomic markers, long-term environmental datasets, physiological and omics measurements, and high-throughput phenotyping platforms are now routine components of modern breeding programs. Paradoxically, while plant breeding now operates in a multimodal data ecosystem, most prediction models remain effectively unimodal, relying on a single type of information at a time-genomics alone, enviromics alone, physiology alone, or phenomics alo
papers.summary: This perspective argues that modern plant breeding generates multimodal data (genomics, enviromics, phenomics) but prediction models still largely use only one data type at a time, limiting accuracy. The authors advocate for integrating these diverse data streams into unified prediction frameworks to fully leverage the information now routinely collected. This shift could significantly improve breeding efficiency and crop improvement.

papers.title: Developing a Specialized Dravet Syndrome Ontology for Rare Disease Informatics and AI Applications.
papers.abstract: Dravet syndrome (DS) is a severe developmental and epileptic encephalopathy whose clinical and research representation requires integration of heterogeneous knowledge spanning seizures, development, behavior, SUDEP/autonomic risk, genetics, comorbidities, electrophysiology, pharmacology, and drug responsiveness. We report the development of a DS-focused ontology created by expert-guided specialization of a previously published epilepsy ontology. Scope expansion was defined through a scientific advisory board, structured review meetings, and iterative ontology curation in OWL. The resulting res
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: This paper describes the creation of a specialized ontology for Dravet syndrome, a severe form of epilepsy, by refining a broader epilepsy ontology with expert input. The ontology aims to integrate diverse clinical and research data to support rare disease informatics and AI applications. This resource could improve data sharing, analysis, and machine learning for Dravet syndrome research.

papers.title: HeartBioPortal 3.0: an integrated cardiovascular genomics knowledge environment for molecular, clinical and population-scale interpretation.
papers.abstract: Cardiovascular genomics is producing rapidly expanding genetic, molecular, phenotypic and clinical data, yet relevant evidence remains fragmented across resources and difficult to translate into actionable biological and ultimately translational knowledge. HeartBioPortal (HBP) is a browser-based cardiovascular knowledge environment that was developed to address this problem by organizing omics, variant, phenotype and clinical evidence centered around gene queries. Here we describe HBP 3.0, a major update that expands both the data architecture and interpretive interface. This update introduces
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: HeartBioPortal 3.0 is an updated web-based platform that integrates diverse cardiovascular genomics data—including genetic variants, molecular profiles, and clinical information—into a unified, gene-centered knowledge environment. This major expansion improves data architecture and interpretive tools, making it easier for researchers and clinicians to translate fragmented evidence into actionable biological and translational insights.

papers.title: RD-OMICS: An Integrative Multi-Omics Data Inventory in Rare Diseases.
papers.abstract: Rare diseases (RD) impact over 30 million individuals in the United States, yet fewer than 5% of the identified conditions have FDA-approved treatments. Progress in RD research is hindered by small patient cohorts, biological heterogeneity, and the fragmented, inconsistently annotated publicly available omics data, which limits integrative analysis and translational discovery. Here, we present RD-OMICS, a data inventory with integrated and structured RD omics data from Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO), in the form of a knowledge graph. We developed a metadata harmonization pipeline that combines
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: RD-OMICS is a new knowledge graph that integrates and harmonizes rare disease omics data from public repositories like GEO, addressing the fragmentation and inconsistent annotation that hinder research. This resource aims to enable integrative analysis and translational discovery for rare diseases, where fewer than 5% have approved treatments. By structuring previously scattered data, it provides a unified platform for researchers to explore multi-omics information in rare disease contexts.

papers.title: A knowledge graph and multi-agent reinforcement learning model for psychological risk identification and intervention in online learning.
papers.abstract: As the scale of online learning expanded, students tended to exhibit declining learning behaviors and accumulating task backlogs during self-directed study. These issues further led to reduced learning motivation and increased psychological pressure. To address this, this study constructed an innovative model that integrated a knowledge graph with multi-agent reinforcement learning. The model enabled learning state risk identification and personalized intervention. The study conducted a systematic evaluation using comprehensive learning behavior data from seven selected courses. The results in
papers.summary: This study developed a model combining knowledge graphs with multi-agent reinforcement learning to identify psychological risks in online learners and provide personalized interventions. Using data from seven courses, the model effectively detected declining motivation and task backlog, offering a new approach to supporting student well-being in digital learning environments.

papers.title: Eras of bioinformatics technologies from command-line interfaces to artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots.
papers.abstract: Over the past 75 years, especially the recent quarter century, bioinformatics has undergone a profound transformation, evolving from a specialized field of command-line tools to a cornerstone of modern biomedical and life sciences. We trace this journey through distinct technological eras driven by exponential biological data growth and parallel computational advances. The genomic revolution established foundational sequence analysis tools and was rapidly followed by the next-generation sequencing era, when unprecedented data volumes shifted the bottleneck from generation to analysis. This dro
papers.summary: This review traces the evolution of bioinformatics over 75 years, from command-line tools to AI chatbots, highlighting how exponential biological data growth and computational advances have driven distinct technological eras. The genomic revolution established foundational tools, and the next-generation sequencing era shifted the bottleneck from data generation to analysis.

papers.title: Integrative proteogenomic analyses identify plasma proteins that impact the risk of ischemic stroke.
papers.abstract: Understanding the proteins implicated in the pathogenesis of ischemic stroke is important for elucidating disease mechanisms and informing prevention strategies. In this study, we aim to identify plasma proteins with a potentially causal effect on risk of ischemic stroke by integrating the largest available genetic data sources for plasma proteins, ischemic stroke and its risk factors.
papers.summary: This study uses genetic data to identify plasma proteins that may causally influence the risk of ischemic stroke, helping to clarify disease mechanisms and potential prevention targets. By integrating large-scale genetic datasets for proteins, stroke, and risk factors, the authors pinpoint specific proteins with likely causal roles.

papers.title: MetagenomicKG: a knowledge graph for metagenomic applications.
papers.abstract: The sheer volume and variety of genomic content within microbial communities makes metagenomics a field rich in biomedical knowledge. To traverse these complex communities and their vast unknowns, metagenomic studies often depend on distinct reference databases, such as the Genome Taxonomy Database (GTDB), the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG), and the Bacterial and Viral Bioinformatics Resource Center (BV-BRC), for various analytical purposes. These databases are crucial for the genetic and functional annotation of microbial communities. Nevertheless, the inconsistent nomenclatur
papers.summary: MetagenomicKG is a new knowledge graph that integrates multiple major metagenomic reference databases (GTDB, KEGG, BV-BRC) to help researchers navigate the complex and vast genomic content of microbial communities. By unifying these resources, it addresses the challenge of inconsistent nomenclature and enables more coherent analysis across different data sources.

papers.title: AART enables fast and accurate cross-platform proteomic translation.
papers.abstract: Plasma proteomic profiling has been widely used for biomarker discovery, disease prediction and diagnosis, and patient stratification. However, technical differences across assay platforms often result in low-to-moderate agreement, limiting study reproducibility, data integration, and model transferability. Here we present AART, a cross-platform proteomic translation framework that integrates matched-protein ridge regression with proteome-wide residual learning. We benchmarked AART spanning three independent cohorts profiled using three major platforms, including Olink, SomaScan, and mass spec
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: AART is a new computational framework that translates protein measurements across different assay platforms (Olink, SomaScan, mass spec) with high accuracy, addressing the long-standing problem of poor cross-platform reproducibility in plasma proteomics. By combining ridge regression with residual learning, it enables better data integration and model transferability for biomarker discovery and disease prediction.

papers.title: GlyGen: a knowledgebase linking glycan data with protein and gene data to reveal novel biological connections.
papers.abstract: Glycans influence protein structure and function, modulate tissue development, regulate organ function, drive pathogen interactions, control inflammation and immunity, and impact most disease processes. However, glycan and glycosylation data are frequently difficult to access, existing across heterogeneous resources, with inconsistent representation and limited linkage to the genes, proteins, and protein sites that define their biological context. This fragmentation limits integration with genomics, proteomics, and other omics data, and constrains the discovery of glycan-mediated relationships
papers.summary: GlyGen is a new knowledgebase that integrates glycan data with protein and gene information, making it easier to discover biological connections involving glycans. By linking fragmented glycan data to genes, proteins, and protein sites, it enables integration with genomics and proteomics for broader biological insights.

papers.title: Artificial intelligence optimization model for public safety in smart cities based on multi-source data collaboration in the internet of things.
papers.abstract: The prevalent cross-departmental data barriers and ineffective public safety risk management and control in smart city construction severely restrict governance efficiency. This study takes Hefei as an empirical case and innovatively proposes and validates a "technology-institution-data" ternary collaborative smart city public safety artificial intelligence optimization model. The core contribution lies in constructing a three-layer conceptual framework of "perception-decision-value", systematically integrating real-time perception driven by the Internet of Things (IoT), intelligent decision-m
papers.summary: This paper presents an AI optimization model for public safety in smart cities that integrates IoT-driven real-time perception with intelligent decision-making, using Hefei as a case study. The model addresses cross-departmental data barriers by proposing a 'technology-institution-data' ternary collaborative framework. While the work is relevant to smart city governance, it does not introduce novel bioinformatics methods or findings.

papers.title: Mechanistically Informed and Omics-Guided Essential Oil Applications for Food Preservation.
papers.abstract: The growing demand for safe, sustainable, and clean-label food preservation strategies has accelerated interest in plant-derived essential oils (EOs) as natural antimicrobial agents. These chemically complex mixtures exhibit broad-spectrum activity against foodborne pathogens and spoilage microorganisms through multitarget mechanisms, including membrane disruption, metabolic interference, oxidative stress induction, and modulation of quorum sensing and biofilm formation. However, their practical application remains constrained by compositional variability, physicochemical instability, matrix-d
papers.summary: This review discusses how essential oils (EOs) can be used as natural antimicrobials for food preservation, focusing on their multi-target mechanisms like membrane disruption and biofilm inhibition. It highlights challenges such as compositional variability and instability, and suggests that omics-guided approaches could help optimize their application.

papers.title: Targeting mir130b-IL-33-PD-L1 axis effectively inhibits esophageal squamous carcinoma progression.
papers.abstract: We used an integrated approach combining molecular biology techniques, animal experiments, and clinical samples to elucidate the mechanistic basis of interleukin-33 (IL-33)-induced programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) upregulation and its role in squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC) progression. Multi-omics analyses and clinical datasets identified that increased expression of IL-33 and its receptor in tumor tissues correlated with advanced stage and poor prognosis. IL-33 expression was associated with enhanced migration, invasion, and proliferation of tumor cells, and these effects were partly reverse
papers.summary: This study identifies a new molecular pathway in esophageal squamous cell carcinoma where IL-33 signaling upregulates PD-L1, promoting tumor progression. Using multi-omics and clinical data, the authors show that targeting the mir130b-IL-33-PD-L1 axis could be a potential therapeutic strategy. The findings link immune checkpoint regulation with tumor aggressiveness in this cancer type.

papers.title: Quantitative phosphoproteomic profiling of CCL5/CCR5 signaling cascade in melanoma cells.
papers.abstract: The CCL5/CCR5 axis plays a pivotal role in tumor progression and metastasis. We previously reported CCR5 promoted melanoma EMT and metastasis by upregulating TGFβ1 expression via the PI3K/AKT/GSK3β pathway. However, the full spectrum of downstream events triggered by CCR5 activation remains poorly understood.
papers.summary: This study uses quantitative phosphoproteomics to map the signaling cascade downstream of CCL5/CCR5 activation in melanoma cells, revealing a broader set of phosphorylation events beyond the previously known PI3K/AKT/GSK3β pathway. The findings provide a more comprehensive view of how CCR5 signaling promotes tumor progression and metastasis.

papers.title: Enhancing medical Q&A systems with multimodal knowledge graphs and dual-layer attention mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Medical intelligent question-answering (QA) systems have become important tools for improving the efficiency of healthcare services, and recent research has increasingly emphasized performance optimization and multimodal integration. However, existing systems still face several challenges in intent recognition, entity extraction, and multimodal knowledge fusion, particularly reduced accuracy in multi-label classification, heavy reliance on large-scale annotated data, and limited support for cross-modal retrieval. To address these issues, this study proposes a medical intelligent QA framework t
papers.summary: This paper proposes a medical intelligent question-answering framework that uses multimodal knowledge graphs and dual-layer attention mechanisms to improve intent recognition, entity extraction, and cross-modal retrieval. The approach aims to overcome limitations in existing systems such as reduced accuracy in multi-label classification and heavy reliance on large-scale annotated data.

papers.title: Microbial Ecology at the Nexus of Food Safety and Biotechnology With Ecological Mechanisms, Risks, and Emerging Innovations.
papers.abstract: Food systems are complex microbial ecosystems in which microorganisms play dual and often contrasting roles as agents of foodborne contamination and as essential drivers of food production and biotechnological innovation. Microbial ecology provides an integrative framework for understanding how microbial interactions, environmental conditions, and human interventions shape food safety outcomes and technological processes. This narrative integrative review is aimed at synthesizing current literature on microbial ecology at the nexus of food safety and food biotechnology and at identifying key r
papers.summary: This review examines how microbial ecology connects food safety and biotechnology, highlighting the dual role of microorganisms as both potential contaminants and essential tools in food production. It synthesizes current knowledge on microbial interactions and environmental factors that influence food safety outcomes and biotechnological processes.
